[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Benin-demography.png/330px-Benin-demography.png)](/wiki/File:Benin-demography.png)

Demographics of Benin. Data from [FAO](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization "Food and Agriculture Organization"), 2005. Number of inhabitants in thousands.

The demographics of [Benin](/wiki/Benin "Benin") include [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density"), [ethnicity](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group"), education level, health of the populace, economic status, religious affiliations and other aspects of the population.

The majority of Benin's 13.3 million people live in the south.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) The population is young, with a life expectancy of 62 years.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

About 42 African ethnic groups live in this country; these various groups settled in Benin at different times and have also migrated within the country.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) Ethnic groups include:

* the [Yoruba](/wiki/Yoruba_people "Yoruba people") in the southeast (migrated from what is now [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") in the 12th century);[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)
* the [Dendi](/wiki/Dendi_people "Dendi people") in the north-central area (they came from what is now [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali") in the 16th century);[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)
* the [Bariba](/wiki/Bariba_people "Bariba people") and the [Fula (or Fulani)](/wiki/Fula_people "Fula people") ([Fula](/wiki/Fula_language "Fula language"): *Fulɓe*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Peul*) in the northeast;[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)
* the [Betammaribe](/wiki/Betammaribe "Betammaribe") and the [Somba](/wiki/Somba_people "Somba people") in the [Atacora Range](/w/index.php?title=Atacora_Range&action=edit&redlink=1 "Atacora Range (page does not exist)");[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)
* the [Fon](/wiki/Fon_people "Fon people") in the area around [Abomey](/wiki/Abomey "Abomey") in the South Central;[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) and
* the [Mina](/wiki/Mina_people "Mina people"), [Xweda](/w/index.php?title=Xweda&action=edit&redlink=1 "Xweda (page does not exist)"), and [Aja](/wiki/Aja_(people) "Aja (people)") (who came from what is now [Togo](/wiki/Togo "Togo") in the 12th century) on the coast.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)
* the Ewe people on the coast

[French](/wiki/French_language "French language") is the official language but is spoken more in urban than in rural areas.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) The literacy rate is 54% among adult males and 31% among adult females as of 2018;[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) these rates are slowly growing.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Recent migrations have brought other African nationals to Benin, including Nigerians, Togolese and Malians.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) The foreign community also includes many Lebanese and Indians involved in trade and commerce.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) The personnel of the many European embassies, foreign aid missions, nongovernmental organizations and missionary groups account for much of the 5,500 European population.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

Several religions are practiced in Benin.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) [Traditional African religions](/wiki/Traditional_African_religions "Traditional African religions") are widespread (50%), and their practices vary from one ethnic group to the other.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) Arab merchants introduced Islam in the north and among the Yoruba.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) European missionaries brought [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") to the south and central areas of Benin.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") account for 20% of the population and Christians for 30%.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) Many nominal Muslims and Christians continue to practice traditional African religion traditions.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) It is believed that [West African Vodun](/wiki/West_African_Vodun "West African Vodun") originated in Benin and was introduced to Brazil and the Caribbean Islands by slaves taken from this particular area of the Slave Coast.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

## Population

[Population](/wiki/Population "Population"), [fertility rate](/wiki/Fertility_rate "Fertility rate") and [net reproduction rate](/wiki/Net_reproduction_rate "Net reproduction rate"), United Nations estimates

According to the 2022 revision of the [World Population Prospects](/wiki/World_Population_Prospects "World Population Prospects")[[3]](#cite_note-UN_WPP-3)[[4]](#cite_note-UN_WPP_2022-4) the total population of Benin was 12,996,895 in 2021, compared to only 2,255,000 in 1950. The proportion of children below the age of 15 in 2010 was 43.7%; 53.3% were between 15 and 65 years of age, while 3% were of 65 years or older.[[5]](#cite_note-WPP_2010-5)

## Ethnic groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Benin&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ethnic groups")]

There are several dozen **ethnolinguistic groups** in Benin, representing three of Africa's language families: [Niger–Congo](/wiki/Niger%E2%80%93Congo_languages "Niger–Congo languages"), [Nilo-Saharan](/wiki/Nilo-Saharan_languages "Nilo-Saharan languages"), and [Afroasiatic](/wiki/Afroasiatic_languages "Afroasiatic languages"). The latter is represented by [Hausa](/wiki/Hausa_people "Hausa people"), living mostly as merchants in the north, while Nilo-Saharan is represented by the Dɛndi people, descending from the [Songhai Empire](/wiki/Songhai_Empire "Songhai Empire"). The [Songhay](/wiki/Songhay_languages "Songhay languages") language [Dɛndi](/wiki/Dendi_language "Dendi language") predominates along the [Niger River](/wiki/Niger_River "Niger River") in the far north, and is used as a [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") in Muslim areas throughout the north, in Alibori, Borgou, and Donga provinces. Of the Niger–Congo family, five branches are represented:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Femme_et_le_plaisir_d%27%C3%AAtre_belle%28Peuhl%29_du_Benin%29_04.jpg/250px-Femme_et_le_plaisir_d%27%C3%AAtre_belle%28Peuhl%29_du_Benin%29_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Femme_et_le_plaisir_d%27%C3%AAtre_belle(Peuhl)_du_Benin)_04.jpg)

Fula woman in Benin.

* [Mande](/wiki/Mande_languages "Mande languages") by the [Boko](/w/index.php?title=Boko_people&action=edit&redlink=1 "Boko people (page does not exist)") or Busa, now in the far eastern corner (southern Alibori-northern Borgou), but previously more widely spread before being largely absorbed by the Bariba
* [Senegambian](/wiki/Senegambian_languages "Senegambian languages") by the nomadic [Fulani](/wiki/Fulani "Fulani") scattered across the northeast
* [Benue–Congo](/wiki/Benue%E2%80%93Congo_languages "Benue–Congo languages") by the [Yoruba](/wiki/Yoruba_people "Yoruba people") such as those of the old kingdom of [Sakete](/wiki/Sakete "Sakete"), and the capital city of [Porto-Novo](/wiki/Porto-Novo "Porto-Novo"), having expanded west from the Yoruba cities of [Oyo](/wiki/Oyo,_Nigeria "Oyo, Nigeria") and [Ife](/wiki/Ife "Ife") in the 12th to 19th centuries
* [Gur (Voltaic) languages](/wiki/Gur_languages "Gur languages") predominate in the four northern provinces, with the [Batɔmbu (Bariba)](/wiki/Bariba_people "Bariba people") of the old [Borgou (Bariba) Kingdom](/wiki/Borgu "Borgu") occupying most of the countryside in its successor provinces of Borgou and Alibori, as well as the provincial capital of [Parakou](/wiki/Parakou "Parakou"); the [Yom](/wiki/Yom_language "Yom language") throughout much of Donga province and its capital [Djougou](/wiki/Djougou "Djougou"); and several groups in the Atakora, including the [Bɛtamaribɛ](/w/index.php?title=Ditamari&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ditamari (page does not exist)") of the Otammari country around the provincial capital of [Natitingou](/wiki/Natitingou "Natitingou"), the Biali, the [Waama](/wiki/Waama "Waama") of [Tanguiéta](/wiki/Tangui%C3%A9ta "Tanguiéta"), and the [Gulmàceba](/wiki/Gurma "Gurma").
* [Kwa](/wiki/Kwa_languages "Kwa languages"), especially the [Gbe languages](/wiki/Gbe_languages "Gbe languages") spoken by the [Tado](/wiki/Tado "Tado") peoples in the southern and central provinces: the [Aja](/wiki/Aja_people "Aja people") who established themselves in Kouffo province from neighboring [Togo](/wiki/Togo "Togo") and gave rise to the other Tado peoples of Benin, except for the [Mina](/wiki/Mina_people "Mina people") of Mono province, who arrived separately from Togo or [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana"): The [Fɔn](/wiki/Fon_people "Fon people") culture centered in Zou province around the old Fɔn capital of [Abomey](/wiki/Abomey "Abomey"), but also dominant in [Cotonou](/wiki/Cotonou "Cotonou") and southern Atlantique areas such as [Ouidah](/wiki/Ouidah "Ouidah"); the [Maxi](/w/index.php?title=Maxi,_Collines&action=edit&redlink=1 "Maxi, Collines (page does not exist)") in central Collines, especially around [Savalou](/wiki/Savalou "Savalou"); the [Ayizɔ](/w/index.php?title=Ayizo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ayizo (page does not exist)") of central Atlantique ([Allada](/wiki/Allada "Allada")); the [Xwla](/w/index.php?title=Xwla&action=edit&redlink=1 "Xwla (page does not exist)") and [Xueda](/w/index.php?title=Xueda&action=edit&redlink=1 "Xueda (page does not exist)") in the lagoons of the coast; the [Tɔfin](/w/index.php?title=Tofin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tofin (page does not exist)") of Ouémé; and the [Gun](/wiki/Gun "Gun"). Other Kwa languages are spoken by the [Anii](/wiki/Anii "Anii") in southern Donga in the region of [Bassila](/wiki/Bassila "Bassila"), and the [Fooɖo](/wiki/Foodo "Foodo") in western Donga near the town of [Ouaké](/wiki/Ouak%C3%A9 "Ouaké").

The largest ethnic group are the [Fon](/wiki/Fon_people "Fon people"), with 1.7 million speakers of the Fon language (2001), followed by the various [Yoruba groups](/wiki/Beninois_Yoruba "Beninois Yoruba") (1.2 million), the Aja (600,000), the Bariba (460,000), the Ayizo (330,000), the Fulani (310,000), and the Gun (240,000). Near the ports in the south can be found many people who are descended from returned [Brazilian](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") slaves.[[15]](#cite_note-17)

## Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Benin&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Languages")]

Main article: [Languages of Benin](/wiki/Languages_of_Benin "Languages of Benin")

Over 50 languages are spoken in Benin. The official language is [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"). Of the many indigenous [African languages](/wiki/African_languages "African languages"), [Fon](/wiki/Fon_language "Fon language") and [Yoruba](/wiki/Yoruba_language "Yoruba language") are the most important in southern Benin. In the north there are at least six major languages, including [Baatonum](/wiki/Baatonum_language "Baatonum language") and [Fulani](/wiki/Fula_language "Fula language").

## Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Benin&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Religion")]

Religion in Benin (ARDA 2020 est.)[[16]](#cite_note-18)

1. [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") (23.5%)
2. [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") (8.01%)
3. [Other Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (12.4%)
4. [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (29.1%)
5. [Ethnic Religion](/wiki/African_traditional_religions "African traditional religions") (26.6%)
6. [Irreligion](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") (0.23%)
7. Other (0.16%)

:   [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Benin "Islam in Benin") 27.7%, [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") 25.5%, [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") 13.5% ([Celestial](/wiki/Celestial_Church_of_Christ "Celestial Church of Christ") 6.7%, [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") 3.4%, other Protestant 3.4%), [Vodoun](/wiki/Vodoun "Vodoun") 11.6%, other Christian 9.5%, other [traditional religions](/wiki/Traditional_African_religion "Traditional African religion") 2.6%, Other 2.6%, None 5.8%, (2013 est.)
:   Pew Forum estimated that, in 2010, that [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") constituted 30% of the country, [Protestants](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") 23.2%, and other Christians 0.3%, with all Christians making up 53.4% of the population.
